title: Heralded ultrafast generation of macroscopic quantum states in matter with bright squeezed vacuum light
abstract: We show that bright squeezed vacuum light, combined with a single-shot quadrature measurement of the post-interaction light, enables the ultrafast generation of macroscopic quantum states in matter. Although in the weak-coupling regime multiphoton quantum light leaves the unconditional matter state as a classical mixture due to light--matter entanglement, quadrature-based heralding prepares the matter in a Gaussian-weighted quantum superposition. For an ensemble of resonantly electric-dipole-coupled two-level systems, this heralding dynamics acts as a Gaussian filter with respect to the electric polarization, with brighter squeezed-vacuum light accelerating the preparation of the zero-eigenvalue Dicke state. Counter-rotating terms further drive a stroboscopic transition from this Dicke state to a cat-like state. Our results open a route to ultrafast engineering of macroscopic quantum matter with strong-field quantum light.
summary: This paper demonstrates how using bright squeezed vacuum light and single-shot quadrature measurements can rapidly generate macroscopic quantum superpositions in matter. By analyzing the dynamics within the Tavis–Cummings and Dicke models, the authors show that measurement acts as a quantum filter, driving the system into highly entangled, cat-like states. This opens a pathway for ultrafast quantum state engineering using strong-field quantum light.
main_problem: The paper addresses the ultrafast generation of macroscopic quantum states in matter using light-matter interactions.
main_result: Quadrature-based heralding allows for the preparation of matter in a quantum superposition, driving the system from a Dicke state to a cat-like state.
method: The analysis employs the Tavis–Cummings and Dicke models, utilizing the external-field approximation and single-shot quadrature measurements to condition the matter state.
model_or_system: The system involves matter (modeled by two-level systems/spin operators) coupled to a quantum light field, specifically bright squeezed vacuum (BSV) light. The dynamics are governed by the Tavis–Cummings and Dicke Hamiltonians.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), Spin Wigner function, and the matter density matrix $ho_m(t)$.
important_parameters: Squeezing parameter ($\xi$), effective field strength ($F_c$), and particle number ($N$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the external-field approximation (XFA) and neglects relaxation or dephasing effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate QFI time profiles and Wigner functions, showing the transition from Dicke states to cat-like states under strong fields, and comparing heralded vs. unconditional dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first analyzing unconditional dynamics (showing classical mixtures) and then introducing quadrature-based heralding to prepare quantum superpositions. It extends this to the Dicke model to show the final transition to cat-like states.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed theoretical framework for engineering macroscopic quantum states in matter using non-classical light sources and measurement feedback, which is central to modern quantum optics and AMO research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Quadrature-heralded matter dynamics in the TC model (19) within the XFA for 𝐹c ≈0.10 (𝑔= 0.005, 𝑟= 3) and 𝑁= 32. (a) Time profile of the QFI density FQ/𝑁(blue solid line), for ˜𝑞= 0 [Eq. (25)]. The orange line shows the unconditional case calculated from ˆ𝜌m [Eq. (12)]. The light-blue dashed line shows the large-𝑁limit (26). (b)–(d) Spin Wigner function 𝑊(𝜃, 𝜙) [Eq. (23)] at the times indicated in (a). For visualization, only the 𝑧axis is inverted. (e) QFI dynamics for different quadrature outcomes, ˜𝑞= 𝑞/𝑔= 0, 100, 200, corresponding to 𝑞= 0, 0.5, 1 for 𝑔= 0.005.

Figure 2: Dependence of the quadrature-heralded QFI density FQ/𝑁on the measurement resolution Δ ˜𝑞 in the TC model (19) within the XFA for 𝐹c ≈0.10 (𝑔= 0.005, 𝑟= 3), ˜𝑞= 0, and 𝑁= 32. The QFI is calculated from ˆ𝜌𝑞;Δ𝑞[Eq. (28)]. Given 𝑔= 0.005, the resolutions Δ ˜𝑞= Δ𝑞/𝑔= 40, 80, 120 correspond to Δ𝑞= 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, respectively.

Figure 2 shows the time evolution of the QFI density FQ/𝑁[Eq. (22)] calculated from the matter state ˆ𝜌𝑞;Δ𝑞[Eq. (28)] heralded by a finite-resolution quadrature measurement. Since the scaled outcome ˜𝑞= 𝑞/𝑔 is the relevant quantity in the XFA (18), the dynamics depends on Δ𝑞only through the scaled resolution Δ ˜𝑞= Δ𝑞/𝑔. We set the outcome center to ˜𝑞= 0. Numerically, the integral over 𝑞′ is evaluated using the rectangular method. Overall, the monotonic growth of the ideal measurement Δ ˜𝑞= 0 is suppressed up to a certain time by finite Δ ˜𝑞, but eventually crosses over to the same squared-exponential growth. At early times, larger Δ ˜𝑞prolongs the agreement with the unconditional curve...

Figure 3: Comparison between the XFA and full light–matter simulations for the quadrature-heralded QFI density FQ/𝑁in the TC model (19). The blue dashed curve shows the XFA result (25), and the solid curves show full numerical simulations for different electron–photon couplings 𝑔= 0.005, 0.01, 0.015, and 0.02. Parameters are set to 𝑟= 3 −ln(𝑔/0.005), 𝑞= 0, Δ𝑞= 0, and 𝑁= 32.

Figure 4: Quadrature-heralded matter dynamics in the Dicke model (39) within the XFA for ˜𝑞= 0 and 𝑁= 32. (a) Time profile of the QFI density FQ/𝑁for 𝐹c = 𝑔exp(𝑟) ≈0.27 and 0.037, corresponding to 𝑟= 4 and 2 for 𝑔= 0.005. (b) Spin Wigner function 𝑊(𝜃, 𝜙) at the time indicated in (a).
